title: How decisions are saved on disk
question: How should decisions be stored — in one big growing file, or split into many?
rationale: One small JSON file per decision, now filed in a sub-folder per app version (decisions/v1.0/…), flat within each version, so you can open a release folder and see exactly what shipped. This is the by-folder layout first rejected (o10c) — but grouping by VERSION is safe where stage/topic wasn't: a cut release is immutable, so the folder never has to be relabeled. The per-decision version field (d36) stays the source of truth; the folder just mirrors it, and numbering stays global.

history.from: One small file per decision in a single flat folder (no sub-folders)
history.reason: Moved each decision into a sub-folder named for the app version it shipped in (decisions/v1.0/…), flat within each version, so a release's decisions can be browsed as a folder. The earlier no-folders choice avoided a tree that drifts when labels change (o10c) — but versions don't drift, a released version is fixed, so that objection doesn't apply. The version field (d36) remains the source of truth; the folder mirrors it and global numbering is unchanged.

options.label: Sub-folders by version, flat within each

tradeoffs.note: open a release folder (decisions/v1.0/) and see exactly what shipped in it
a version is immutable once cut, so the folder never needs relabeling — unlike stage/topic (o10c)
version now lives in both the folder name and the field (d36); the generator keeps them in sync
generator walks sub-folders and writes relative paths into the manifest; the viewer is unchanged

options.label: One small file per decision in one flat folder

tradeoffs.note: each decision is a small, readable file with clean change-history
a flat folder copes past dozens without a giant file
rework the script and tools to read a folder, plus a one-time move
scroll one ~680-line file; every change touches the whole thing
becomes unwieldy as decisions pile up
the folder tree mirrors the stage framework
changing a stage or topic means moving files; the tree drifts from the labels
